hypothesis: For datasets with heavy-tailed graph-size distributions (e.g., AFLOW), an online adaptive batching algorithm that updates node/edge padding targets via an exponentially weighted moving average of recent batch statistics and enforces a minimum real-graph fraction of 0.9 will, within the first 100,000 training steps on a single A100 GPU using the PaiNN model with batch size 32, reduce the mean combined training time per step by at least 25% compared to both static-64 and the current dynamic algorithm while producing a final test RMSE after two million steps that is not statistically worse (p>0.05) than the better of those two baselines.
